title: The Debt Illusion: What Fiat Economics Is Really Defending
body_html:
A lot of anti-deflation rhetoric sounds like concern for workers and stability. Often it is really concern for debt structures that become fragile once money stops melting. The essay presses on this uncomfortable point: if falling prices threaten the system, maybe the problem is not falling prices but a civilization built on nominal promises that cannot withstand honest accounting.

Debt contracts fixed in nominal terms are vulnerable when purchasing power rises. Wages can be sticky. Balance sheets can break. But those are institutional facts, not universal laws proving that cheaper goods are somehow immoral. They reveal a system that has normalized leverage, rollover dependence, and political pressure to rescue bad capital structures.

Once you see that, fiat policy starts to look less like neutral stabilization and more like an ongoing defense of particular arrangements. Asset holders, governments, and over-indebted institutions all have reasons to fear a world in which money hardens and prices tell the truth.

The essay’s challenge is therefore political: do we want an economy organized around protecting debt or around rewarding productivity? A great deal of macroeconomic doctrine is really an attempt to avoid answering that question openly.
